text:
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST — ONCOLOGIC SURVEILLANCE

CLINICAL INDICATION: 59-year-old female with history of BRAF V600E-mutant stage IV cutaneous melanoma, metastatic to liver, bilateral axillary and right inguinal lymph nodes, and subcutaneous tissues of the left flank. Prior treatment with dabra/Mekinist (progressed), followed by Yervoy/Opdivo (discontinued ipi after grade 3 colitis), now on nivo maintenance monotherapy. Most recent prior imaging reportedly demonstrated no evidence of active metastatic disease. Surveillance scan.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following intravenous administration of 100 mL of iohexol contrast (Omnipaque 350). Axial reconstructions at 5 mm and 1.25 mm slice thickness were obtained. Coronal and sagittal reformats reviewed. No prior outside imaging available for direct side-by-side comparison; review performed with reference to clinical history and prior report description.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and airways: The lungs are clear. No focal consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pleural-based lesion identified. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. The trachea and central airways are patent and midline.

Mediastinum and hila: The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. A few small mediastinal lymph nodes are seen measuring up to 7 mm in short axis, within normal limits.

Axilla: A small left axillary lymph node is again noted, now measuring approximately 0.5 cm in short axis. This is stable compared to the prior examination. Given the clinical history, this likely represents residual fibrotic or reactive tissue. No new ipsilateral or contralateral axillary adenopathy. The right axilla is clear.

Chest wall and osseous structures: No suspicious chest wall lesion or soft tissue mass. Osseous structures demonstrate no focal lytic or sclerotic lesion suspicious for metastatic involvement. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and contour. No focal hepatic lesion is identified on this examination. Previously described hypodense lesions in segments VI, II, and IVa are no longer visualized. The hepatic parenchyma enhances homogeneously without discrete nodule or mass. The intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated.

Biliary system: Gallbladder is present, thin-walled, without cholelithiasis. Common bile duct not dilated.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No splenic lesion.

Pancreas: Unremarkable. No ductal dilatation or focal mass.

Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are unremarkable. No adrenal nodule.

Kidneys and ureters: Both kidneys are normal in size with symmetric nephrographic enhancement. A few bilateral renal cortical cysts, the largest measuring approximately 1.4 cm on the right, are simple in appearance and unchanged. No hydronephrosis.

Aorta and vasculature: The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber without aneurysmal dilatation. No lymphadenopathy in the retroperitoneal, aortocaval, or mesenteric distributions. A few small mesenteric lymph nodes are present, measuring up to 6 mm, nonspecific.

Gastrointestinal: The stomach, small bowel, and visualized large bowel are unremarkable in appearance without wall thickening or obstruction. Mild sigmoid diverticulosis without pericolonic inflammatory change.

---

PELVIS:

Lymph nodes: No pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy identified. A previously noted right inguinal lymph node is not discretely identified on this examination; no lymph node meeting size criteria for enlargement is seen in this region.

Pelvic organs: The uterus and adnexa appear grossly unremarkable. No pelvic free fluid. Bladder is moderately distended and without wall lesion.

Bony pelvis: No suspicious osseous lesion. Mild degenerative changes of the sacroiliac joints and pubic symphysis.

---

SOFT TISSUES / SKIN:

No suspicious subcutaneous nodule or soft tissue mass is identified along the trunk, flanks, or imaged extremities. No cutaneous thickening concerning for in-transit metastasis. Previously described subcutaneous nodules along the left flank are not visualized.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No hepatic lesion identified; previously described hepatic foci are not visualized on this examination, consistent with continued resolution.

2. Small left axillary lymph node, measuring approximately 0.5 cm, grossly stable compared to prior and likely represents residual fibrotic change in the setting of known prior bulky nodal disease. No new axillary, mediastinal, or hilar adenopathy.

3. No pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy. The previously noted right inguinal lymph node is no longer identified.

4. No subcutaneous nodules along the left flank or elsewhere.

5. No new suspicious finding in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis to suggest recurrent or metastatic melanoma.

6. Incidental sigmoid diverticulosis; mild degenerative spinal changes as noted above.

Overall, imaging appearances are favorable with no evidence of active metastatic disease identified on this surveillance examination. Continued clinical and radiologic follow-up as directed by the treating team.

Transcribed and reviewed by: Attending Radiologist, Body Imaging Division
